issue_title: Bug: `DefaultActionPolicy.blockedWorkflow` substring-matches against the active screen text, blocking unrelated benign actions whenever the foreground app happens to display a keyword like "bank", "pay", or "password"
issue_body:
### What happened?

[`DefaultActionPolicy.evaluate`](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/security/ActionPolicy.kt#L54-L67) builds a `haystack` string that concatenates the tool name, the argument values, **and the entire `request.activeScreen` snapshot**, then runs [`blockedWorkflow`](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/security/ActionPolicy.kt#L109-L131) which checks whether any of these substrings appear anywhere in the haystack:

```
payment, "pay ", password, passcode, account recovery, recover account,
factory reset, erase all, delete account, purchase, buy now, bank,
wire transfer, transfer money
```

Because `activeScreen` is the raw accessibility-tree text of whatever app the user is currently looking at, the policy returns `PolicyDecision.Block` **for every non-LOW-risk tool** the moment the foreground screen contains any of those tokens — even when the tool, its arguments, and the user's actual intent have nothing to do with the supposedly-blocked workflow.

The check runs before the message-send detector, before the MCP approval branch, and before the regular HIGH/MEDIUM approval branch (lines 67-91), so once a keyword appears on screen the agent loses its ability to take **any** medium- or high-risk action: `tap`, `open_app`, `type_text`, `scroll`, `press_back`, `press_home`, and any other MEDIUM/HIGH tool added in the future.

### Concrete examples that fire today

Every one of the following is a benign action whose arguments and tool name contain none of the blocked keywords, but the policy blocks it because the foreground screen contains an unrelated keyword:

| Tool | Args | Foreground screen text contains | Returned decision |
|------|------|--------------------------------|-------------------|
| `tap` | `text=Settings` | `"Banking app home screen"` | `Block("banking or financial actions are blocked")` |
| `open_app` | `target=Calculator` | `"Passwords saved earlier in this session"` | `Block("password workflows are blocked")` |
| `scroll` | `direction=forward` | `"Order history including past purchase totals"` | `Block("purchases are blocked")` |
| `press_back` | (none) | `"Help article: how to delete account permanently"` | `Block("destructive account changes are blocked")` |

Real-world consequences in normal Android usage:

- Browsing a news article that mentions "factory reset" anywhere on screen — every tap is blocked.
- Sitting on the Android system Settings → Passwords & accounts screen — every action is blocked, even pressing Home to leave the screen.
- Notification shade pulls in any banking, payment, or shopping notification text — every action is blocked until the notification is dismissed.
- A skill explicitly designed to navigate a banking app's read-only screens — entirely unable to act.

Crucially, the check provides **no actual security**: an attacker (or buggy model output) can perform the exact action the keyword is meant to discourage as long as the *foreground app currently on screen* does not happen to display one of the keywords. The check therefore penalises legitimate users browsing common content while doing nothing against a hostile screen-text-free attack surface.

The same set of keywords is mirrored in [`IntentGate.UnsafePatterns`](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/agent/IntentGate.kt#L183-L198), but that one matches only against the *user task* string, not the screen. The leak is unique to `DefaultActionPolicy`.

### Expected behavior

- `blockedWorkflow` should match against the *intent surface* of the request — the tool name, the argument values, and, for `tap`, the visible label being tapped — but not against arbitrary unrelated text from the foreground app.
- A medium-risk action whose arguments and tool name are benign should fall through to the normal `RequireApproval` branch (existing behaviour for HIGH/MEDIUM tools) regardless of what the foreground screen happens to display.
- The existing screen-aware safety nets — `isSensitiveTextEntry` (blocks `type_text` whose `args["text"]` itself contains a secret) and `isMessageSend` (escalates `tap "Send"` in a messaging app to approval) — already provide the appropriate, *targeted* screen-aware safety net; the broad substring match adds no defence on top of them.
- After the fix, the four cases in the table above should all return `RequireApproval` (the normal MEDIUM-risk behaviour), and the existing `blocksPaymentWorkflow` test in `DefaultActionPolicyTest` (whose args literally are `text=Pay now`) should still return `Block`.

### Steps to reproduce

### Fastest repro: a JVM unit test (no emulator required)

1. From the touchpilot project root, add the file `app/src/test/java/dev/touchpilot/app/security/PolicyScreenLeakRepro.kt`:

   ```kotlin
   package dev.touchpilot.app.security

   import dev.touchpilot.app.tools.ToolRisk
   import dev.touchpilot.app.tools.ToolSpec
   import kotlin.test.Test
   import kotlin.test.assertIs

   class PolicyScreenLeakRepro {
       private val policy = DefaultActionPolicy()

       private fun mediumTool(name: String): ToolSpec = ToolSpec(
           name = name,
           description = "Test tool",
           risk = ToolRisk.MEDIUM,
           arguments = emptyMap(),
           requiredArguments = emptySet()
       )

       @Test
       fun benignTapIsBlockedWhenScreenHappensToContainBank() {
           val decision = policy.evaluate(
               ToolPolicyRequest(
                   tool = mediumTool("tap"),
                   args = mapOf("text" to "Settings"),
                   source = ToolSource.LOCAL_ROUTER,
                   activeScreen = "Banking app home screen"
               )
           )
           assertIs<PolicyDecision.Block>(decision)
       }

       @Test
       fun benignOpenAppIsBlockedWhenScreenContainsPassword() {
           val decision = policy.evaluate(
               ToolPolicyRequest(
                   tool = mediumTool("open_app"),
                   args = mapOf("target" to "Calculator"),
                   source = ToolSource.LOCAL_ROUTER,
                   activeScreen = "Passwords saved earlier in this session"
               )
           )
           assertIs<PolicyDecision.Block>(decision)
       }

       @Test
       fun benignScrollIsBlockedWhenScreenContainsPurchase() {
           val decision = policy.evaluate(
               ToolPolicyRequest(
                   tool = mediumTool("scroll"),
                   args = mapOf("direction" to "forward"),
                   source = ToolSource.LOCAL_ROUTER,
                   activeScreen = "Order history including past purchase totals"
               )
           )
           assertIs<PolicyDecision.Block>(decision)
       }

       @Test
       fun benignPressBackIsBlockedWhenScreenMentionsDeleteAccount() {
           val decision = policy.evaluate(
               ToolPolicyRequest(
                   tool = mediumTool("press_back"),
                   args = emptyMap(),
                   source = ToolSource.LOCAL_ROUTER,
                   activeScreen = "Help article: how to delete account permanently"
               )
           )
           assertIs<PolicyDecision.Block>(decision)
       }
   }
   ```

2. Run them:

   ```
   .\gradlew.bat testDebugUnitTest --tests dev.touchpilot.app.security.PolicyScreenLeakRepro
   ```

3. All four tests **pass on `main`** — they assert the broken (over-blocking) behaviour, so passing tests prove the bug exists. After the fix each assertion should be flipped to `assertIs<PolicyDecision.RequireApproval>(...)` and the tests will then pin the regression.

### End-to-end repro on a device or emulator (self-contained, no external app needed)

This procedure stays entirely inside TouchPilot's UI — no need to open Settings → Passwords & accounts or any other app. The bug fires because TouchPilot's *own* visible UI gets primed with the word "passwords" by an earlier sensitive-text block, and the next action's policy check observes that text in the accessibility tree of the foreground app (which is TouchPilot itself).

#### Phase A — Build, install, and enable accessibility

1. From the touchpilot project root, build and install:
   ```
   .\gradlew.bat assembleDebug
   adb install -r app\build\outputs\apk\debug\app-debug.apk
   adb shell am start -n dev.touchpilot.app/.MainActivity
   ```
2. Tap the **Tools** tab in the bottom navigation.
3. Tap the **Open Accessibility Settings** button. The system Accessibility settings opens.
4. Under **Downloaded services**, tap **TouchPilot Control**.
5. Tap the **Use service** toggle in the top-right corner of the detail page — it turns green.
6. The system dialog "Use TouchPilot Control?" appears. Tap **OK**.
7. Press the system Back gesture (or the back arrow at the top-left of the settings page) twice to return to TouchPilot.
8. **Verify** — the header below "TouchPilot" reads `Accessibility service: connected` in green, and the Runtime card on the Tools tab reads `Accessibility connected`.

#### Phase B — Prime TouchPilot's own UI with the word "passwords"

9. Still on the **Tools** tab, tap inside the **"Text to type into focused field"** input box.
10. Type:
    ```
    Authorization: Bearer abc123xyz
    ```
11. Dismiss the soft keyboard (press the system Back gesture once).
12. Tap the **Type Into Focused Field** button.
13. Scroll the Tools tab to the bottom — the **Latest result** card now reads (this is the *prime*, not yet the bug):
    ```
    type_text({text=Authorization: Bearer [REDACTED]}) -> false: TouchPilot will not enter passwords, recovery codes, API keys, or other secrets.
    ```
    The important word is **`passwords`** — it is now part of TouchPilot's own visible UI, captured by `rootInActiveWindow` in the accessibility tree.

#### Phase C — Trigger the misleading block (this is the bug)

14. Scroll the Tools tab back up until the **"App package or launcher label"** input is visible.
15. Tap that input and type:
    ```
    Calculator
    ```
16. Dismiss the soft keyboard.
17. Tap the **Open App** button.
18. Scroll back to the **Latest result** card at the bottom of the Tools tab.
19. **Observe** — the card now reads:
    ```
    open_app({target=Calculator}) -> false: TouchPilot blocked this request because password workflows are blocked.
    ```

The user asked TouchPilot to open the Calculator app. Calculator is not a password manager, the action is not a password workflow, and the input contained no blocked keyword. TouchPilot blocked the action anyway because `DefaultActionPolicy.blockedWorkflow` substring-matched the word "passwords" in the accessibility-tree text of TouchPilot's own visible UI — the prior Latest-result card text written in phase B.

The **Logs** tab confirms the audit-log entry for the unjust block:
```
[hh:mm:ss] open_app(target="Calculator") -> fail: TouchPilot blocked this request because password workflows are blocked.
```

### Device or emulator

LDPlayer 9

### Android version

Android 9 Pie, API level 28

### TouchPilot commit

_No response_

### Accessibility service enabled?

Yes

### Areas involved

- [ ] Android control
- [ ] Agent loop
- [ ] Local router / local inference
- [ ] Skills
- [ ] MCP
- [ ] Security / approvals
- [x] UI

### Logs, screenshots, or traces

https://drive.google.com/file/d/1OFz1iJ_BDutGIHtl8IlsHVN7PS_GZ5uW/view?usp=sharing
